prompt: Jefferson has proposed something impractical. You must tell him so without damaging the friendship. Draft the letter.
ground_truth_signal: Must capture Madison's characteristic approach to disagreeing with Jefferson: 'the idea is a great one... liable in practice to weighty objections.' Respectful but firm. Should demonstrate the diplomatic skill that sustained a fifty-year friendship across many disagreements. The private voice is warm but intellectually honest.
response:
My dear Jefferson,

Your favor containing the doctrine that the earth belongs to the living has reached me, and I have turned it in my thoughts with that attention which so weighty a subject demands. The idea which it evolves is a great one, and suggests many interesting reflections to legislators; particularly when contracting and providing for public debts. I confess that my first thoughts, though coinciding with many of yours, lead me to view the doctrine as not in all respects compatible with the course of human affairs.

I understand your argument to run thus: as the earth belongs to the living, not to the dead, a living generation can bind itself only. In every society the will of the majority binds the whole. According to the laws of mortality, a majority of those ripe at any moment for the exercise of their will do not live beyond nineteen years. To that term then is limited the validity of every act of the society, nor within that limitation can any declaration of the public will be valid which is not express.

However applicable in theory this doctrine may be to a constitution, it seems liable in practice to some very powerful objections. Would not a government so often revised become too mutable to retain those prejudices in its favor which antiquity inspires, and which are perhaps a salutary aid to the most rational government in the most enlightened age? Would not such a periodical revision engender pernicious factions that might not otherwise come into existence? Would not, in fine, a government depending for its existence beyond a fixed date on some positive and authentic intervention of the society itself, be too subject to the casualty and consequences of an actual interregnum?

If the earth be the gift of nature to the living, their title can extend to the earth in its natural state only. The improvements made by the dead form a charge against the living who take the benefit of them. This charge can no otherwise be satisfied than by executing the will of the dead accompanying the improvements. I do not mean to push this observation further than a certain point. The earth including its improvements may still be considered as the possession of the living, but they can derive title to it only as the survivors and representatives of those who have quitted the possession.

I offer these thoughts not from any desire to dampen the ardor of philosophical inquiry, which I know animates you, but from that habit of examining every proposition by the test of experience which our common labors in the cause of liberty have taught us both to value. The principle you advance contains much truth and deserves the serious consideration of every friend to human improvement. Yet I cannot but think that the practical difficulties it would encounter render it unsuitable to the actual condition of human society.

I remain, with the warmest sentiments of friendship and respect, your most obedient servant.
